Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Nordic International Limited

Facts.Text: Nordic International Limited was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands on 2007-01-16 to purchase a fishing trawler to be converted and equipped to operate as a seismic survey vessel.

Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd, of which Morten was a director and shareholder, was appointed to be the manager of the Vessel pursuant to a ship management agreement dated 1 January 2007 between Nordic Maritime and Nordic International.
On 2007-07-04, Nordic International Limited entered into a shareholders' agreement between Sinwa Limited and Morten Innhaug under which each party would own 50 percent of the company's shares.
Sinwa Limited’s rights were later novated to Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd by a novation agreement dated 28 August 2007.
Following the shareholders' agreement, the board of Nordic International Limited consisted of two nominees from Sinwa and two nominees from Morten Innhaug, including Morten himself.
Pursuant to the shareholder’s agreement, Sinwa injected US$2m working capital into Nordic International.
In September 2007, Sinwa guaranteed a term loan of US$16m extended by OCBC to Nordic International for the conversion of the Vessel.
On 2007-06-08, a time charterparty was entered into under which Nordic International Limited chartered the vessel to BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd for a minimum of three years.
In August 2008, BGP indicated a wish to Morten Innhaug to cancel the Time Charter due to operational problems and a market downturn.
Morten Innhaug emailed BGP proposing a compensation structure and suggesting that the operation of the vessel be transferred to Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd.
A tripartite Memorandum of Agreement was signed on 23 August 2008 by BGP, Nordic Maritime and TGS, relieving BGP of its obligations and transferring rights to Nordic Maritime.
Morten Innhaug incorporated Nordic Geo-Services Ltd on 10 September 2008 to take over the Time Charter from BGP.
A Notice of Assignment dated 22 September 2008 purported to assign the Time Charter from BGP to Nordic Geo-Services Ltd.
By a letter dated 23 October 2008, Sinwa informed Morten that it objected to the assignment and alleged a breach of the requirement for unanimous agreement on non-technical matters.
TGS sent Nordic Maritime a notice of termination of the seismic services agreement in December 2008, and the vessel was subsequently laid up.
Nordic Maritime continued to pay the operating costs of the Vessel even after it was laid up, amounting to approximately $50,000 per month.
BGP objected to the 'reinstatement' of the Time Charter, taking the view that its obligations had ceased following the signing of the MOA.
Morten’s counsel informed Nordic International's representatives that they had no authority to commence legal proceedings against BGP without Morten's consent under the shareholders' agreement.
Clause 40 of the Time Charter granted the charterer the option to purchase the Vessel with equipment at US$5m after the charter period.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the structuring and implementation of the transfer of time charter arrangements, and the subsequent handling of enforcement steps, created a conflict between the interests of Nordic International Limited and entities controlled by Morten Innhaug.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors are required to manage the company's business in its best interests, avoid conflicts with personal or related-entity interests, and refrain from using company-related opportunities for personal gain without informed approval.
Party_Details.Application: Nordic International Limited's revenue depended on its vessel's time charter. After the charterer sought cancellation, Morten Innhaug negotiated a structure where his own entities, Nordic Maritime and Nordic Geo-Services, became the beneficiaries of seismic services revenue and a vessel purchase option. These steps were taken without formal board approval from Nordic International Limited. When the revenue stream ceased, the company was unable to receive further hire payments, while Morten Innhaug used his position to object to enforcement actions against the original charterer.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the legal nature of the Memorandum of Agreement and the Notice of Assignment, distinguishing between assignment of contractual benefits and transfer of burdens, and concluded that the description of the Notice of Assignment as an assignment of the time charter was a misnomer. It held that only Nordic International Limited could assign the benefit of the time charter and that BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd could only assign its use of the vessel under clause 17(a), not the time charter itself. The court treated the two 'assignments'—of the time charter and of the seismic services agreement—as a single transaction and focused on the economic effect: BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd was removed from the relationship with TGS-NOPEC Geophysical Company SA while remaining, on paper, liable to Nordic International Limited, and Nordic Geo-Services Ltd, controlled by Morten Innhaug, obtained the increased seismic revenue and the option to purchase the vessel. The court found that Morten Innhaug 'procured' the purported assignment in the sense of bringing it about, regardless of which party initiated discussions, because he negotiated with BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd and TGS-NOPEC Geophysical Company SA, signed the Memorandum of Agreement on behalf of Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd, and signed the acknowledgment of the Notice of Assignment on behalf of Nordic Geo-Services Ltd. Applying fiduciary principles, the court held that Morten Innhaug owed duties to act bona fide in the best interests of Nordic International Limited, to avoid conflicts of interest, not to profit from his position without informed consent, and not to enter into self-dealing transactions. It concluded that he breached these duties by structuring the arrangements so that Nordic Geo-Services Ltd and Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd captured the seismic services revenue and the purchase option while Nordic International Limited's rights under the time charter were exposed to challenge. The court rejected his argument that Nordic International Limited was 'no worse off' because the time charter terms remained unchanged and BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd remained liable on paper, pointing to the actual cessation of charter hire from December 2008 and BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd's reliance on the Memorandum of Agreement to deny liability. The court emphasised that breach of fiduciary duty is actionable per se and that liability to account for profits does not depend on proof of loss. On conflict of interest, the court held that the fact that Nordic International Limited was a ship owner and Nordic Geo-Services Ltd a charterer did not eliminate the possibility of conflict. It relied on authorities such as Premier Waste Management Ltd v Towers and Re Allied Business and Financial Consultants Ltd to state that it is irrelevant that the company could not or would not have taken the opportunity or that the opportunity lay outside the company's usual business. The court found that Morten Innhaug placed himself in a position where his personal and related-entity interests in the seismic services agreement and the purchase option conflicted with Nordic International Limited's interest in enforcing the time charter and preserving its charter hire stream. It also characterised the arrangement as self-dealing because Morten Innhaug effectively substituted Nordic Geo-Services Ltd for BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd as charterer, thereby acting on both sides of the transaction. Regarding the withholding of charter hire, the court found that Morten Innhaug's conduct in initially preventing Nordic International Limited from pursuing arbitration against BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd, including his counsel's 2009-06-25 letter and his vote against arbitration at the 2011-10-24 board meeting, was inconsistent with acting in Nordic International Limited's best interests. The court accepted that he was concerned about preserving his relationship with BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd and about Nordic Geo-Services Ltd's indemnity exposure under the Notice of Assignment, and that he sought to create a deadlock to trigger the shareholders' agreement deadlock clause so he could buy out Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd and then pursue BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd himself. These findings supported the conclusion that he caused or contributed to the withholding of charter hire. On the option to purchase the vessel, the court noted that the time charter granted the charterer an option to purchase the vessel at US$5,000,000 after three years, that a 2010 valuation placed the vessel at about US$30,000,000, and that the assignment structure would have allowed Nordic Geo-Services Ltd to exercise the option. Although Morten Innhaug ultimately did not exercise the option and later stated he no longer intended to do so, the court held that his acquisition of the right to exercise the option through the assignment constituted an unauthorised profit opportunity and a breach of the no-profit and no-conflict rules. The court rejected Morten Innhaug's reliance on section 391 of the Companies Act. It held that the statutory relief requires that the director acted honestly and reasonably and that it is fair to excuse him. The court found that his conduct displayed surreptitiousness, including excluding Sinwa-nominated directors from negotiations and structuring documents to avoid Nordic International Limited being a party, and that he intended to secure benefits for himself and his related entities. It concluded that he did not act honestly or reasonably and that this was not an appropriate case for relief. On ratification, the court distinguished between ratification of the transaction and release of the director from liability. It held that any acceptance by Nordic International Limited of charter hire payments from Nordic Geo-Services Ltd or failure to notify OCBC of objections did not amount to informed shareholder consent to release Morten Innhaug from liability. The court relied on correspondence, including Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd's 2008-10-23 letter and its subsequent pursuit of a derivative action, to find that Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd consistently objected to the conflict and did not ratify the breaches. For remedies, the court ordered an account of profits in respect of gains made by Morten Innhaug through the seismic services arrangements, noting evidence that he expected to earn a margin of US$5,000 to US$7,000 per day after costs between August and December 2008. For the loss of charter hire, the court treated the appropriate remedy as equitable compensation and held that the loss depended on the outcome of the pending arbitration between Nordic International Limited and BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd, including issues such as lay-up reductions under clause 5(d) of the time charter and netting of expenses. Drawing on authorities concerning contingent losses and third-party claims, the court ordered that the assessment of compensation for loss of charter hire be reserved until after the conclusion of the arbitration, with liberty to apply. It fixed costs of the action in favour of Nordic International Limited and directed that each party bear its own costs for an earlier injunction application.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Morten Innhaug

Facts.Text: Morten Innhaug incorporated Nordic International Limited in the British Virgin Islands in 2007 to purchase and convert a fishing trawler into a seismic survey vessel.
Morten Innhaug was a director of Nordic International Limited and also controlled Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd.
Morten Innhaug secured a time charterparty for the vessel with BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd at a rate of US$37,000 per day.
In August 2008, Morten Innhaug discussed the potential cancellation of the time charter with the charterer and explored transferring rights to his own entities.
On 19 August 2008, Morten Innhaug proposed to the charterer that the operation of the vessel be transferred to Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd.
Morten Innhaug signed a Memorandum of Agreement on behalf of Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd providing for the transfer of rights from the original charterer.
Morten Innhaug incorporated Nordic Geo-Services Ltd to take over the charter position from BGP.
In his capacity as CEO of Nordic Geo-Services Ltd, Morten Innhaug acknowledged the Notice of Assignment of the Time Charter.
Nordic Maritime continued to pay approximately $50,000 per month for the vessel's operating costs even after it was laid up.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Morten Innhaug's conduct in facilitating the transfer of commercial benefits to his own entities and his influence over the company's legal enforcement decisions constituted a breach of his fiduciary duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who control multiple related entities must ensure that the economic interests of a company are not subordinated to those related entities, and that material changes to revenue-generating contracts are approved with full disclosure.
Party_Details.Application: Morten Innhaug held board control in both the vessel-owning company and the entities that received assigned charter benefits. Following the charterer's request to cancel, he implemented a structure that redirected seismic revenue and a purchase option to his personal companies. He subsequently opposed the company's attempts to commence arbitration to recover unpaid hire from the original charterer.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined Morten Innhaug's roles in Nordic International Limited, Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd, and Nordic Geo-Services Ltd, and his control over the structuring of the time charter and seismic services arrangements. It found that he was the 'controlling mind' of the related entities and that he orchestrated the Memorandum of Agreement and the Notice of Assignment to substitute Nordic Geo-Services Ltd for BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd as the effective charterer while capturing the increased seismic services revenue and the vessel purchase option. The court held that this amounted to self-dealing and placed him in a position of conflict between his personal and related-entity interests and the interests of Nordic International Limited. The court rejected his contention that he merely responded to BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd's initiative, holding that he 'procured' the arrangements by negotiating terms, signing the Memorandum of Agreement, and signing the acknowledgment of the Notice of Assignment. It emphasised that he did not involve Sinwa-nominated directors in the negotiations and that Nordic International Limited was not made a party to the key documents, despite being the owner and time charter counterparty. The court inferred that this structure was chosen to avoid potential objections from Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd and to secure benefits for Nordic Maritime Pte Ltd and Nordic Geo-Services Ltd. In assessing whether he acted in the best interests of Nordic International Limited, the court noted that the time charter provided a secure three-year revenue stream that underpinned Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd's decision to invest and guarantee the bank loan. It found that Morten Innhaug did not negotiate compensation from BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd for early termination, despite the time charter's provisions, and instead focused on preserving his relationship with BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd and obtaining the seismic services agreement and purchase option for his related entities. The court concluded that this approach exposed Nordic International Limited's rights under the time charter to challenge and led to the cessation of charter hire from December 2008. Regarding the withholding of charter hire, the court found that Morten Innhaug's actions in instructing his counsel to challenge the authority to commence arbitration against BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd and in voting against arbitration at the 2011-10-24 board meeting were motivated by concerns about his ongoing relationship with BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd, Nordic Geo-Services Ltd's indemnity obligations under the Notice of Assignment, and his plan to trigger the deadlock clause to buy out Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd. The court held that these motivations showed that he prioritised his own and his related entities' interests over Nordic International Limited's interest in promptly enforcing its charter hire claim. On the purchase option, the court observed that the assignment structure would have allowed Nordic Geo-Services Ltd to exercise the option to buy the vessel at US$5,000,000, significantly below the 2010 valuation of about US$30,000,000. It considered his later indication of intention to exercise the option and concluded that acquiring control over this option through the assignment was a significant personal benefit obtained without Nordic International Limited's informed consent. The court applied section 391 of the Companies Act and concluded that Morten Innhaug did not satisfy the requirements for relief. It found that his conduct involved deliberate exclusion of co-directors, complex structuring to avoid Nordic International Limited's direct involvement, and pursuit of personal advantages, which did not meet the standard of acting 'honestly and reasonably'. The court also held that there was no effective ratification or release by Nordic International Limited, as Sinwa SS (HK) Co Ltd consistently objected to the arrangements and pursued a derivative action. Ultimately, the court held Morten Innhaug liable for breaches of his fiduciary duties in procuring the purported assignment, causing the withholding of charter hire, and obtaining the benefit of the purchase option. It ordered an account of profits in respect of gains from the seismic services arrangements and reserved the assessment of equitable compensation for loss of charter hire until after the conclusion of the arbitration between Nordic International Limited and BGP Geoexplorer Pte Ltd, with liberty to apply.
